rows.v: To cook food quickly in a small amount of fat over relatively high heat, tossing or stirring frequently. A foundational fast-cooking method.
She sautéed the mushrooms in butter until golden, tossing them so they browned evenly.
From French 'sauter' (to jump) — the food literally 'jumps' in the pan as a skilled cook flicks it, the classic toss of the sauté.
To cook tough cuts slowly in a covered pot with a little liquid, after first searing. Low, slow, moist heat that turns tough meat tender.
He braised the short ribs for three hours until they fell apart at the touch of a fork.
From French 'braiser', linked to 'braise' (hot coals) — the dish was traditionally cooked over and under embers in a covered pot.
To briefly boil food then plunge it into ice water, stopping the cooking. Sets color, loosens skins, and softens slightly.
She blanched the green beans for a minute, then shocked them in ice water to keep them vivid green.
From French 'blanchir' (to whiten) — originally used to whiten or pre-cook foods; the quick boil-and-shock preserves color and texture.
To add liquid (wine, stock) to a hot pan to dissolve the browned bits stuck to the bottom, turning them into the base of a sauce.
After searing the steak, he deglazed the pan with red wine, scraping up the flavorful fond.
From French 'déglacer' (to unfreeze, melt). The 'glaze' of caramelized juices is dissolved back into liquid — pure concentrated flavor.
To combine two liquids that don't normally mix (like oil and vinegar) into a smooth, stable blend. The science behind mayonnaise and vinaigrette.
She slowly whisked oil into the egg yolk to emulsify it into a thick, glossy mayonnaise.
From Latin 'emulgere' (to milk out) — an emulsion suspends tiny droplets of one liquid throughout another, like the fat in milk.
To simmer a liquid so water evaporates, concentrating its flavor and thickening it. Patience turns thin sauce into intense glaze.
He let the sauce reduce by half until it coated the back of a spoon and the flavor deepened.
From 'reduce' in its sense of making smaller — the volume shrinks as water boils off, leaving a more concentrated, flavorful result.
To brown the surface of food quickly at high heat, building a flavorful crust through caramelization. The first step of countless dishes.
She seared the scallops hard for ninety seconds a side, building a deep golden crust.
From Old English 'sēar' (dry, withered) — high heat dries and browns the surface. The crust's flavor comes from the Maillard reaction.
To cook food gently in liquid kept just below a simmer. The delicate heat suits eggs, fish, and fruit without toughening them.
He poached the eggs in barely-trembling water until the whites set around still-runny yolks.
From Old French 'pochier' (to pocket) — said to describe how a poached egg's white forms a 'pocket' around the yolk.
To chop food into very small, fine pieces. The finest standard chop, used for garlic, herbs, and aromatics.
She minced the garlic so finely it nearly melted into the sauce.
From Old French 'mincier' (to cut small), from Latin 'minutia' (smallness) — the same root behind 'minute' and 'diminish'.
To cut food into long, thin matchstick strips. A classic knife cut for vegetables, prized for its uniform, elegant shape.
He julienned the carrots into delicate matchsticks for the stir-fry.
A French term, possibly from a chef or cook named Jean Julien; it has denoted thin matchstick cuts in cookbooks since the 18th century.
To cut food into tiny, precise cubes (about 1–3mm), usually by julienning first then dicing. The finest classic dice.
The brunoise of peppers added a confetti of perfectly even, tiny cubes to the garnish.
From French, derived from Brunoy, a town near Paris. One of the most exacting cuts in classical knife work — a test of precision.
To slice leafy herbs or greens into thin ribbons, by stacking, rolling, and cutting. A delicate cut for basil, mint, or greens.
She rolled the basil leaves and sliced a fine chiffonade to scatter over the dish.
From French 'chiffon' (rag, ribbon) — the technique produces thin, ribbon-like strips resembling soft fabric shreds.
To cut food into uniform cube shapes. Sizes range from fine to large, and evenness ensures even cooking.
He diced the onion into neat, even cubes so it would cook uniformly.
From Old French 'des' (plural of 'de', a die for gaming) — the cubes resemble the six-sided dice used in games.
'Mise en place' — having all ingredients prepped, measured, and arranged before you start cooking. The discipline that makes service run smoothly.
Her mise en place was immaculate: every ingredient chopped and bowled before the first pan hit the heat.
French for 'putting in place'. The professional kitchen's golden rule — preparation before action — credited with separating calm cooks from chaotic ones.
To cut the bones out of fish or meat, producing a boneless piece. Also the boneless cut itself.
The chef filleted the whole fish in seconds, sliding the knife cleanly along the bones.
From Old French 'filet' (a thread, little band) — the boneless strip of flesh. Pronounced 'fih-LAY' for the French cut, 'FILL-it' in British usage.
To cook food, usually meat or vegetables, with dry heat in an oven, browning the exterior while cooking through. A cornerstone method.
She roasted the chicken at high heat until the skin turned crackling and golden.
From Old French 'rostir' — originally cooking on a spit over fire, now generally dry-heat oven cooking. The browning builds deep, savory flavor.
'Grilling' cooks over direct heat from below; 'broiling' cooks under direct heat from above. Both use intense, direct heat to char and brown.
He grilled the steaks over the coals, then finished the cheese topping under the broiler.
'Grill' from Old French 'graille' (a grate); 'broil' from Old French 'bruler' (to burn) — both capture cooking by fierce, direct radiant heat.
To cook food with the vapor from boiling water, keeping it moist and preserving nutrients and color. A gentle, fat-free method.
She steamed the dumplings over simmering water until the wrappers turned translucent.
From Old English 'stēam' (vapor) — the food never touches the water, cooking purely in the rising hot vapor. Prized for keeping vegetables vivid.
'Deep frying' submerges food in hot oil; 'shallow frying' uses a thin layer. High heat crisps the exterior fast.
They deep-fried the chicken until shatteringly crisp, then shallow-fried the next batch in less oil.
From Old French 'frire', from Latin 'frigere' (to roast, fry). The crisp, golden crust comes from rapid moisture loss and browning in hot fat.
To cook food sealed in a bag, submerged in precisely temperature-controlled water for a long time. Delivers perfect, edge-to-edge doneness.
He cooked the steak sous vide at 130°F for two hours, then seared it for a perfect medium-rare throughout.
French for 'under vacuum' — the food is vacuum-sealed and cooked in a precise water bath. A modern technique embraced by both pros and home cooks.
To cook sugars (in sugar itself or in foods like onions) until they brown and develop deep, sweet, complex flavor.
She caramelized the onions low and slow for an hour until they turned jammy and golden brown.
From 'caramel', via Spanish/Portuguese, possibly from Latin 'calamus' (reed/sugar cane). The browning of sugar at high heat creates hundreds of new flavor compounds.
To slowly melt the fat out of meat (like bacon or duck) over gentle heat, leaving crisp solids and usable liquid fat.
He rendered the bacon slowly so the fat melted out and the strips crisped without burning.
From Old French 'rendre' (to give back, yield) — the heat coaxes the meat to 'yield up' its fat, separating liquid from solid.
A cooked paste of equal parts flour and fat, used to thicken sauces and soups. The base of countless classic dishes.
She whisked a blond roux of butter and flour as the foundation for the creamy sauce.
French for 'reddish-brown', describing the color it takes as it cooks. Longer cooking deepens it from white to blond to brown, each with a distinct flavor.
A flavor base of diced onion, carrot, and celery, slowly cooked to build depth in soups, stocks, and braises. The aromatic foundation of French cooking.
Every braise started with a mirepoix sweating gently in the pot to build a savory foundation.
Named after an 18th-century French duke, the Duc de Mirepoix, whose cook is credited with popularizing the aromatic trio.
A flavorful liquid made by simmering bones, vegetables, and aromatics. The backbone of soups, sauces, and braises — distinct from quicker broth.
He simmered the roasted bones for hours to coax out a rich, gelatinous stock.
From the sense of stock as a stored supply or base. A good kitchen keeps stock on hand as the foundation that flavors everything built upon it.
The five classic French 'mother sauces' (Béchamel, Velouté, Espagnole, Hollandaise, Tomato) from which countless other sauces are derived.
Once she mastered the five mother sauces, dozens of derivative sauces became simple variations.
Codified by chef Auguste Escoffier in the early 1900s; they're 'mothers' because every other classic sauce is a 'daughter' built from one of them.
A bundle of herbs (often parsley, thyme, bay) tied together and simmered in a dish to add flavor, then removed before serving.
She dropped a bouquet garni into the simmering stock, fishing it out once it had given up its aroma.
French for 'garnished bouquet' — the herbs are bundled like a small bouquet so they flavor the pot, then lift out cleanly in one piece.
Pasta (or vegetables) cooked until tender but still firm to the bite, not soft. The proper, traditional doneness for pasta.
He drained the pasta a minute early, leaving it al dente with a pleasant bite.
Italian for 'to the tooth' — the pasta offers a slight resistance 'to the tooth', the hallmark of properly cooked pasta in Italian tradition.
The colored outer peel of citrus, grated or peeled to add bright, aromatic flavor without the bitter white pith beneath.
She grated lemon zest over the dish, releasing a burst of fresh citrus aroma.
From the original sense of 'zest' as orange or lemon peel used for flavoring — which gave rise to the figurative 'zest' meaning lively enthusiasm.
To '86' something means it's run out, removed from the menu, or to get rid of it. Universal kitchen shorthand for 'we're out' or 'cancel that.'
'86 the salmon!' the chef called when the last fillet went out the door.
American diner slang of uncertain origin from the 1930s; among many theories, it may be rhyming slang for 'nix'. It spread far beyond kitchens to mean 'get rid of'.
To 'fire' a dish means to start cooking it now. The command that launches a plate or course toward the table.
'Fire table six!' meant the entrées for that table needed to start cooking immediately.
From the sense of igniting or launching into action — 'firing' a dish kicks off its cooking, timed so courses reach the table together.
A dish needed 'on the fly' must be made immediately and fast, usually because something went wrong or a table is waiting. Urgent, rush cooking.
A plate came back wrong, so the chef had to remake it on the fly while the table waited.
From the sense of doing something hurriedly 'while in flight' — without the normal lead time, improvised and rushed under pressure.
To be 'in the weeds' is to be hopelessly overwhelmed with more orders than you can handle. The dreaded state of falling behind during service.
Three big tables sat at once and suddenly the whole line was in the weeds.
A vivid metaphor of being lost and tangled in tall weeds, unable to find your way out — kitchen and restaurant slang for drowning in work.
'All day' is the running total of how many of an item are needed across all current orders. 'Three burgers all day' means three total, right now.
'That's six steaks all day!' the expediter called, tallying every table's order into one number.
Kitchen shorthand for the grand total 'in all' — it cuts through ticket-by-ticket confusion so the line knows the full count at a glance.
Restaurant shorthand for a table that seats a given number — a 'two-top' seats two, a 'four-top' seats four. Basic front-and-back-of-house lingo.
The host seated a two-top by the window and a six-top in the corner booth.
From 'top' meaning a tabletop, with the number indicating its seats — efficient shorthand for communicating table size across a busy restaurant.
